abstract:
Developing highly efficient catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a cornerstone of sustainable solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion. In this work, we uncover the structural and electronic origins of the remarkable 500-fold activity enhancement observed in mixed Ni–Fe oxyhydroxides ($Ni_{1-x}Fe_xOOH$) compared to their pure parent compounds. This synergy creates one of the most effective OER catalysts currently known for alkaline environments. Utilizing operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD), we demonstrate that $Fe^{3+}$ ions in the mixed catalyst occupy octahedral sites characterized by unusually short Fe–O bond distances—a structural compression induced by edge-sharing with the surrounding $[NiO_6]$ octahedra. 

Through density functional theory (DFT+U) calculations, we show that this specific structural motif optimizes the adsorption energies of OER intermediates, significantly lowering the overpotential at the Fe sites. In contrast, we find that the Ni sites remain largely spectators, lacking the catalytic activity required for efficient water oxidation. These findings clarify the long-standing debate over the active species in Ni–Fe systems and provide a blueprint for the rational design of next-generation water-splitting catalysts.
